topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, nauseating knot forming beneath my ribs. I reread the text three times, my eyes blurring slightly as I tried to process the audacity of it. My piece, the one I poured weeks of soul into, was credited to some stranger. A stranger who apparently had the audacity to claim my intellectual property. I slammed my laptop shut so hard the sound echoed in the quiet apartment. My hands were trembling, the knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the desk. I stood up, pacing the small room, the floorboards protesting under my frantic movements. I needed to find the editor, needed to confront this blatant theft. A hot flush crept up my neck, making my cheeks burn. This wasn't just an oversight; this was a deliberate slight, a calculated erasure of my contribution.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the words mocking me from the digital void. A slow, deep burn started in my chest, radiating outward. It felt like a pressure building behind my sternum, making each breath shallow and ragged. "No," I whispered, the sound raspy and devoid of warmth. I started typing furiously, not to compose a rebuttal, but just to release the internal turmoil. Every keystroke was sharp, punctuated by clenched jaw muscles. I threw the keyboard across the room; it hit the wall with a jarring clatter. My breath hitched, short and sharp. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a profound sense of injustice wash over me. This felt fundamentally wrong, a deep violation of trust, and the sheer unfairness of it made my vision swim slightly.

[story 3]

When I finally saw the attribution, the air seemed to evacuate the room. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a sound more animal than human. I picked up the printed copy of the article, the paper feeling flimsy and insulting in my hands. My fingers tightened around the edges until the paper creased. I began to pace the length of the hallway, my footsteps heavy and deliberate, each stride a declaration of displeasure. I stopped abruptly in front of the mirror. My reflection stared back, eyes narrowed, a rigid set to my mouth. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the internal tremor to subside, but the feeling persisted—a fierce, hot churning in my gut that demanded immediate rectification.
